artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <m.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Jasmine <jasmine.rn@glendaledialysis.com>
Date: February 10, 2026
Subject: Rescheduling March PoC meeting & transportation concerns

Hi Jasmine,

I hope you’re doing well. I’m writing because the front desk mentioned my next Plan of Care meeting is scheduled for Monday, 2026-03-02, right after my usual morning treatment. I can keep that date, but I’m worried it might run long, and I’m trying to coordinate a ride.

You probably remember at the June 2023 PoC meeting, I understood most of what Eric and the nurse explained, but I didn’t say anything about how my mom, Rosa Martinez, was just diagnosed with early-stage Alzheimer’s at St. Joseph’s Hospital in May 2023. Since then I’ve been driving from Glendale to Phoenix twice a week to help her with her medications and bills, and that’s when I started missing some treatments.

At my October 2024 PoC meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center, Eric went over my lab results and helped me understand how missing two treatments in September—when I had to take my mom to the ER at Banner – University Medical Center Phoenix for her uncontrolled diabetes—put me at higher risk for fluid overload. I don’t want to repeat that pattern.

Would it be possible on March 2 to keep my regular chair time but move the PoC discussion to a shorter check‑in, or maybe do part of it by phone later that afternoon? My son Luis might be able to drive me, but he needs to clear it with his job.

Thank you for always taking the time to explain things in a way I can understand.

Sincerely,
Maria Elena Rodriguez
8427 W Cactus Bloom Drive
Glendale, AZ 85304

metadata.subject: Rescheduling March PoC meeting & transportation concerns

Hello,

I’m filling out the new patient information packet you mailed me for the annual review, and I got a little confused about the “date” line under the patient information section.

My next Plan of Care review at the Glendale Dialysis Center is scheduled for Friday, March 6, 2026, at 9:30 a.m., but the form just says “patient information > date” without specifying if you want the date I’m completing the form, the date of my upcoming appointment, or the date of my last visit. I was going to put today’s date, 02/27/2026, but I didn’t want to make a mistake and have to redo everything.

I also wanted to mention something for the comments section you added this year. I do feel like my voice matters now, especially after the October 2023 PoC meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center when I finally told the social worker, Ms. Lopez, that I had stopped taking my blood pressure pills for two weeks because I couldn’t afford the $85 copay after my hours were cut at Desert View Dental Clinic.

Please let me know which date you want written there so I can bring the completed forms with me when Jasmine checks me in next week.

Thank you,

Maria Elena Rodriguez
DOB: 04/18/1969
Phone: 602-555-0194

Hi Maria,

Thank you for coming in yesterday for your October 9, 2025 Plan of Care meeting. I know it was a lot of information, especially with all the paperwork updates.

Regarding the “patient information > date” line on the new comments form, you did it correctly when you wrote the date of the meeting (10/09/2025) instead of the date you first received the packet. I also wanted to acknowledge what you shared: during the October 2025 Plan of Care meeting at the Glendale unit, you told the team that if your depression gets worse and you miss more than two treatments in a month like you did in September, your ex-husband Carlos already warned he might go back to court to change the custody schedule for Mateo’s weekend visits because he thinks you’re “too sick” to watch your grandson safely. Drive Mom (Rosa Martinez) to her AA meeting at the community center on 59th Avenue. Meeting starts at 6:00 p.m.; plan to arrive by 5:45 p.m. Bring sweater and water (within fluid limit).

Personal reminder:
- Double-check dialysis paperwork before next day’s treatment.

During my PoC review in November 2024 I asked Eric if we could avoid scheduling treatments on the first Thursday evenings of the month because that’s when I drive my mom, Rosa Martinez, to her AA meetings at the community center on 59th Avenue, and she really depends on me since her relapse back in May 2023. artifacts.content:
From: Nurse Manager <nurse.manager@glendaledialysiscenter.org>
To: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
Date: 2025-03-18 11:21
Subject: March PoC Meeting Summary & Form Instructions

Dear Ms. Rodriguez,

It was good to meet with you on March 17, 2025, for your Plan of Care discussion. I’m summarizing a few key points and clarifying the documentation on the new patient information sheet.

1. Date line on forms
For the field labeled “patient information > date,” please always enter the date of the Plan of Care meeting itself. For example, for yesterday’s visit you correctly wrote 03/17/2025. Do not use your date of birth or the date you first started dialysis.

2. Work and medication concerns
At the March 2025 Plan of Care meeting, you explained that you understood the medication timing changes, but you also told me that if you miss even one shift at Desert View Dental Clinic because you feel faint again after dialysis, your boss Dr. Hernandez said he might have to cut your hours below 16 per week, which would make it impossible to keep up with the $220 monthly payment plan you arranged with Banner Health for your old hospital bill. We’ve documented this to monitor how treatment side effects affect your employment.

3. Next steps
Please bring any updated forms with the date field completed to your treatment on 03/21/2025 so we can scan them into your chart.

Sincerely,

[Name Redacted]
Nurse Manager
Glendale Dialysis Center

metadata.subject: March PoC Meeting Summary & Form Instructions

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@gmail.com>
To: Jasmine <jasmine.rn@glendalekidneycare.com>
Date: Fri, 07 Mar 2025 14:16:32 -0700
Subject: Clarifying PoC meeting notes & dates

Hi Jasmine,

Thank you again for taking the time to sit with me at the Plan of Care meeting this afternoon. I just wanted to write things down while they’re fresh so I don’t mix up the dates later.

You said my next formal PoC review is due in six months, around early September 2025, but that I can ask for an earlier check‑in if my fatigue gets worse. At today’s March 7, 2025 meeting, I explained that when I feel extremely tired on my non‑dialysis days, I sometimes skip picking up my Lasix and blood pressure meds from the Walgreens on W Peoria Avenue because my checking account was overdrawn by $146 at the end of February and I had to choose between the $68 copay and paying my SRP electric bill so my granddaughter Bella could still sleep over on weekends.

Can you please confirm what date you wrote down as the “start” for this new care plan cycle? I wrote “03/07/25” in my little notebook, but I remember the social worker mentioning something about it technically going into effect next Monday. I don’t want to mix that up with my upcoming lab draw on 03/17/25 or the transportation form that’s due by the end of this month.

Sorry for all the questions about dates, but with dialysis on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays, plus my work schedule, I really depend on having the right date in my calendar.

Gracias,
Maria

metadata.subject: Clarifying PoC meeting notes & dates

Date: Mon, 20 Jan 2025 09:41:08 -0700
Subject: Follow-up from 1/18/25 Plan of Care meeting

Dear Ms. Rodriguez,

Thank you for meeting with the interdisciplinary team on Saturday, January 18, 2025, to review your Plan of Care. I’m sending this summary so you have the key dates and recommendations in writing.

1. Plan of Care review date: 01/18/2025. Next formal review is scheduled for approximately 90 days from this date (target week of 04/21/2025). We will confirm the exact appointment time with you closer to that week.

2. Treatment adherence: During your Plan of Care meeting on January 18, 2025, at the Glendale dialysis clinic, you shared that you sometimes skip your Saturday treatments when you have to take your mom, Rosa Martinez, to her chemotherapy appointments at Banner Thunderbird Medical Center. Dr. Patel documented this pattern of missed treatments as “non‑compliance” in the chart, and we discussed potential impact on future disability reviews.

3. Work schedule: You mentioned your current part‑time position at Desert View Dental Clinic and your concern about balancing dialysis days with possible schedule changes. We agreed to revisit this at the next review if your hours change.

4. Next key dates to remember:
   • Lab work: week of 02/10/2025
   • Nutrition check‑in: 02/24/2025
   • Tentative PoC check‑in: late April 2025

Please review this email and let me know if any of the dates or details appear incorrect so we can update your record.

Sincerely,

Ana Lopez, LMSW
Glendale Dialysis Center

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@email.com>

Date: Mon, Feb 10, 2025 3:42 PM
Subject: Follow-up from Feb 3 Plan of Care Meeting

Hi Jasmine,

Thank you again for taking the time with me during the February 3, 2025 Plan of Care meeting at the Glendale center. I went home and filled out that patient feedback form you mentioned. For the question about whether my questions were addressed, I marked “Strongly Agree,” because you and Eric really slowed down and explained the changes to my dry weight and the new schedule for my blood pressure pills.

I did want to share a couple of things I didn’t say in the meeting. In the December 2024 Plan of Care meeting at the Glendale unit, I felt included, but I didn’t tell the social worker that I sometimes leave early on Fridays because my ex-husband Carlos Rodriguez has a no-contact order from the argument we had in April 2023 at my mom’s house in Phoenix, and I have to be across town by 3:00 p.m. to pick up Mateo from his supervised visits at the Maricopa County Family Court building so Luis doesn’t get in trouble again.

Also, at this most recent meeting, I almost mentioned that I sometimes don’t speak up when I’m confused. After my short stay on the behavioral health unit at St. Joseph’s in Phoenix last summer for depression, I worry the team will think I’m “too emotional” or unstable if I ask too many questions.

I trust you, so I wanted you to know this in case it helps explain why I get quiet sometimes. If you think it’s helpful, you can share this with the doctor and social worker.

Gracias,
Maria Elena Rodriguez
Glendale, AZ

Subject: Follow‑up on PoC questions from January meeting

Hi Jasmine,

I finally finished that Plan of Care feedback form they mailed me, but I wanted to clarify how I answered the question about whether my questions were addressed in the January 2026 PoC meeting.

I marked “Agree” instead of the highest option because I did feel like most of my questions were answered, especially about the new fluid limit and the changes to my anxiety meds. You probably remember that in the meeting I explained that I finally called the counseling number Ms. Alvarez gave me after that bad treatment on January 3rd, when I went home to 8427 W Cactus Bloom Drive and drank almost a whole bottle of cheap tequila by myself. Thank you for always listening,

artifacts.content:
Title: Glendale Dialysis – PoC Review & Questions
Date: 2025-01-28 10:00–11:00
Location: DaVita Glendale – Conference Room B
Attendees: Maria Elena Rodriguez; Jasmine (RN); Eric (Dialysis Tech); Ms. Alvarez (Social Worker); NP Carter

Description:

Quarterly Plan of Care review focused on Maria’s understanding of recent changes and time to address her questions.

Agenda:
- Review December lab results and discuss impact of missed bipolar meds.
- Clarify new blood pressure medication timing and fluid restrictions.
- Provide space for Maria to ask follow‑up questions about anxiety, sleep, and transportation.

Notes:
Maria wants to make sure this meeting does not feel rushed. At the last PoC in October 2024 she felt her questions were only “somewhat” addressed because the team ran out of time after talking about her ER visit at Banner Thunderbird. During this January 2025 meeting at the Glendale dialysis center, she plans to bring up how the Plan of Care meeting helped her finally understand why her labs were so bad after she stopped taking her bipolar meds from Dr. Herrera at Banner Behavioral Health for about three weeks in December 2024. She doesn’t want that part widely shared beyond the core team.

Reminder to self: on the feedback form for Q4 (how well questions were addressed), consider choosing an option like “Agree” or “Neither agree nor disagree” if there are still unanswered questions about long‑term transplant evaluation or counseling.

Follow‑up:
- Schedule separate, longer visit with social worker if complex issues remain.
- Ask about written handout summarizing Q&A from this meeting.

From: Ms. Alvarez <salvarez@glendaledialysis.com>
To: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
Date: 2024-06-20 16:12
Subject: June PoC Meeting – Feedback on Questions & Med Changes

Hi Maria,

Thank you for staying a few extra minutes after your June 2024 Plan of Care meeting so we could talk about how things felt for you.

You mentioned on the in‑center survey that your level of agreement for “my questions were addressed during the PoC discussion” was closer to “Agree” than “Completely satisfied,” because you still had some confusion about the evening blood pressure pill on Wednesdays. I appreciated your honesty.

As you told me, you sometimes skip your evening BP pill on Wednesdays so you can drive your mom, Rosa, back to Phoenix from her chemotherapy at St. Joseph’s and still have enough energy for your early Thursday morning dialysis. During the meeting we did go over the medication changes in detail, and you said you liked how clearly the nurse practitioner explained them, but it sounds like you left still feeling only partly reassured about that specific situation.

On your feedback form, it’s perfectly fine that you did not choose the top option for the “questions addressed” item. Marking something like “Agree” or even “Neutral” helps us understand that we need to keep working on this with you.

Let’s plan to revisit this next week in the chair so we can come up with a safer plan that doesn’t leave you torn between your mom’s needs and your own health.

Warmly,

Ms. Alvarez
Renal Social Worker

metadata.subject: June PoC Meeting – Feedback on Questions & Med Changes

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Patient Experience Team <feedback@desertviewdialysis.com>
Date: 2024-12-15 09:48
Subject: December PoC feedback – questions not fully answered

Hello,

I’m writing about the survey I filled out after my December 2024 Plan of Care meeting at Desert View Dialysis. There was a question asking how much I agreed that my questions were addressed during the meeting. I checked the middle option (I think it was something like “Neither agree nor disagree”) because I left feeling mixed.

On the one hand, they did answer my questions about lowering my dry weight, and I appreciated that. On the other hand, I was embarrassed when they brought up in front of the whole team that I had missed two treatments in November when I stayed overnight at my mom Rosa’s apartment in Phoenix after helping her when she fell in the bathroom and refused to go back to Banner Thunderbird Medical Center. I didn’t feel comfortable asking follow‑up questions after that.

Because of that, I didn’t feel right choosing anything like “totally” or “very much” for the questions‑addressed part. It felt more like “somewhat” at best. I still don’t understand what to do if I have to miss again for a family emergency.

If possible, I’d like more private time in my next PoC so I can ask questions without feeling judged in front of everyone.

Thank you for listening,

Maria Elena Rodriguez
MRN: 7749-32586

metadata.subject: December PoC feedback – questions not fully answered

artifacts.content:
Title: PoC Anxiety/Questions Follow‑Up – After March Cannulation Incident
Date: 2024-03-19 13:30–14:15
Location: DaVita Glendale – Chairside, Pod 3
Attendees: Maria Elena Rodriguez; Jasmine (Dialysis Tech); NP Carter

Description:

Short follow‑up focused on Maria’s anxiety and remaining questions after the earlier Plan of Care discussion about adjusting her treatment schedule.

Context:
On March 5, 2024, Maria had a bad cannulation at the DaVita Glendale center that triggered panic attacks. Jasmine stayed late with her for almost 45 minutes after treatment to go over her anxiety meds from Dr. Patel and practice breathing exercises, which helped make the later PoC discussion about changing her treatment schedule easier to understand.

However, Maria reported on her feedback form that her agreement level for “my questions were addressed” was closer to “Somewhat agree” than the top choice. She still has lingering questions about:
- What to do if anxiety spikes mid‑treatment and she wants to stop early.
- Whether it’s safe to drive herself home after a panic episode.
- How these changes might affect her work schedule at Desert View Dental Clinic.

Goals for this visit:
- Review remaining questions one by one and document in chart.
- Provide written instructions so she doesn’t have to rely on memory.
- Reassure Maria that it’s okay not to select the highest satisfaction option if she still feels unsure.

Reminder: Offer to schedule a longer visit with social worker if questions remain only partially answered by the end of this session.

metadata.title: PoC Anxiety/Questions Follow‑Up – After March Cannulation Incident

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@gmail.com>
To: Ms. Alvarez, LCSW <salvarez@glendaledialysis.org>
Date: 2025-02-26 19:42
Subject: Follow-up on February PoC meeting questions

Hi Ms. Alvarez,

I wanted to write down some thoughts about the Plan of Care meeting we had earlier this month before I forget everything. On the feedback form they gave me, there was that question about how much I agree that my questions were addressed. I didn’t mark the highest option because I left the room still feeling a little confused about the dry weight change and some of the lab results.

Later that week Jasmine sat with me after treatment and explained the dry weight change again in Spanish, and then it finally clicked. I told the team at my PoC in February 2025 that I finally understood the dry weight change because Jasmine explained it in Spanish after the meeting, but I also mentioned that my granddaughter Bella’s school counselor at Arrowhead Elementary had called me the week before about her anxiety and bedwetting, and that stress made it hard for me to remember everything they said during the actual meeting.

So on that question about whether my questions were addressed, I was kind of in the middle. Some things were answered, but I needed more time and a calmer mind. If there is an option like “agree” or “somewhat agree,” that feels more honest than saying everything was completely taken care of.

Thank you for listening and for helping me sort through this.

Sincerely,
Maria Elena Rodriguez

metadata.subject: Follow-up on February PoC meeting questions

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@gmail.com>
To: Jasmine (Dialysis Tech) <jasmine@glendaledialysis.org>
Date: 2024-11-30 08:11
Subject: PoC survey question about my questions

Hi Jasmine,

They mailed me that survey about the Plan of Care meeting we had right before Thanksgiving, and I got stuck on the question that asks how much I agree that my questions were answered. I don’t feel comfortable circling the very top choice, because during the actual meeting I was still too shaken up from the panic attack I had driving down Loop 101 to the Glendale center on November 14. 

In the Q1 comments line I considered writing that the Plan of Care meeting we had right before Thanksgiving 2024 helped me because the social worker explained that if I keep missing treatments due to panic attacks like the one I had driving down Loop 101 to the Glendale center on November 14, 2024, I might not qualify for the same transplant list options at Mayo Clinic in Phoenix. That part was clear, but I still had doubts about the anxiety medicine and who I’m supposed to call when I start to feel that way.

So on that item about whether my questions were addressed, I’m leaning toward choosing a middle option instead of the strongest one. I don’t want to sound ungrateful, but it wasn’t perfect either. What do you think is okay to put without making anyone upset?

Thank you for always being patient with me.

Maria

metadata.subject: PoC survey question about my questions

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@gmail.com>
To: Elena Rodriguez <elenarodriguez84@yahoo.com>
Date: 2025-04-05 21:03
Subject: That form they gave me after the April 3 meeting

Mija,

I wanted to tell you about that big Plan of Care meeting they did for me on April 3 at the Glendale facility. It was the one I mentioned where the nurse, the dietitian, the social worker, and the doctor were all there together. They gave me a feedback paper afterward with a question asking how much I agree that my questions were answered during the meeting.

Honestly, I didn’t feel right picking the very top choice. For Q1 I almost wrote that the Plan of Care meeting on April 3, 2025 at the Glendale facility was useful because the nurse finally explained how my high phosphorus might be related to all the stress from taking my mom, Rosa Martinez, to her oncology appointments at Banner Thunderbird Medical Center twice a week since October 2024, and how that’s made me skip my renal diet and snack on fast food in the car. That part I understood, but I still had unanswered questions about what happens if I’m late because of Grandma’s chemo or if they mark me as “noncompliant.”

So when it came to that question about whether my questions were addressed, I was thinking of choosing something like “agree” but not the strongest agreement. I felt heard in some ways, but not completely. I know you always tell me to be honest on those forms, so I’m trying.

Love you,
Mom

metadata.subject: That form they gave me after the April 3 meeting

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <m.rodriguez56@gmail.com>
To: Jasmine <jasmine.rn@glendaledialysis.org>
Date: 2025-03-28 08:42 AM
Subject: Follow‑up on Plan of Care survey

Hi Jasmine,

I hope you’re doing okay and not too worn out from yesterday’s shift. I finished the Plan of Care meeting feedback survey they gave me after treatment, and I wanted to let you know what I put, in case it helps.

For the question about whether the Plan of Care meeting was beneficial to my care, I marked “Strongly Agree.” That really is how I feel. On the Q1 line I wanted to say that the Plan of Care meeting we had in March 2025 at the Glendale clinic really helped me because you and the social worker talked through how my hours got cut from 30 to 18 per week at Desert View Dental Clinic in January 2025 after I missed work for dialysis, and we made a plan so I wouldn’t fall another $600 behind on my SRP electric bill.

On the Q5 comments line I almost wrote that during my PoC meeting on January 9, 2025 at the Glendale Dialysis Center, I was too embarrassed to ask about how my depression and panic attacks since my divorce from Carlos in 2018 are affecting my dialysis because I didn’t want everyone in the room to know I’ve been seeing a counselor at Banner Behavioral Health for weekly sessions and taking sertraline since last summer.

I didn’t put all that in the survey because it felt too personal, but I trust you. Maybe next week, if you have a few minutes while I’m on the machine, we could talk about how to bring this up in a way that doesn’t make me feel so exposed.

Thank you again for always listening.

Love,
Maria

metadata.subject: Follow‑up on Plan of Care survey

artifacts.content:
Subject: Follow-up on PoC Meeting Satisfaction Survey

Hi Maria,

Thank you again for participating in your recent Plan of Care (PoC) meetings and for completing most of the satisfaction survey. I’m following up on Question 2, which asks how much you feel the PoC meetings are beneficial to your care.

On the paper form you turned in after your March 2025 PoC at Fresenius Glendale, you circled that you “AGREE” the meeting was beneficial, but you added several comments in the margins about being unsure on a few points. You mentioned that during the March 2025 PoC meeting, when the team went over your phosphorus labs, you told Eric and me that you sometimes skip your phosphate binders on nights when you work the late shift at Desert View Dental Clinic because you’re afraid of getting diarrhea at the front desk after what happened in October 2023 when you had an accident at work and Dr. Patel wrote you up and warned you your hours could be cut.

You also wrote that you feel the meetings are “helpful but overwhelming,” and that you were leaning more toward “NEITHER AGREE NOR DISAGREE” for Question 2 on the January 18, 2025 PoC because your anxiety spiked.

Before I finalize your survey entry, can you confirm whether you want us to keep your answer for Question 2 as “AGREE,” or if you’d prefer to change it to “SOMEWHAT AGREE” to better reflect your mixed feelings?

No rush—just reply when you’re able.

Best,

Ms. Nguyen, LCSW
Renal Social Worker
Glendale Dialysis Center

metadata.subject: Follow-up on PoC Meeting Satisfaction Survey

artifacts.content:
Subject: Clarifying Your PoC Feedback From January 18

Dear Ms. Rodriguez,

I hope you’re feeling a bit more settled after everything that happened at the January 18, 2025 Plan of Care meeting at the Desert View Dialysis Center in Glendale. I was reviewing your satisfaction form and noticed a discrepancy on Question 2 (how beneficial the PoC meeting was to your care).

Initially, you checked the box for “AGREE,” but then crossed it out and wrote a note that you were closer to “DISAGREE” because you felt too anxious to follow the discussion about your dry weight, blood pressure medications, and treatment time.

You also explained that because of your anxiety and the panic attack you had during the PoC meeting, Dr. Patel had to step out with you into the hallway and increase your lorazepam dose from 0.5 mg to 1 mg before you could come back and finish listening, so you don’t remember everything they explained about the dry weight change. Given that, it makes sense that you might not feel confident saying the meeting was clearly beneficial.

For our records, we’d like to accurately reflect your experience. Would you say your final answer for Question 2 is closer to “SOMEWHAT DISAGREE,” “NEITHER AGREE NOR DISAGREE,” or “SOMEWHAT AGREE”?

Your honest feedback helps us improve how we present information and support you during these meetings.

Sincerely,

Karen Lopez, RN
Nurse Manager
Desert View Dialysis Center

metadata.subject: Clarifying Your PoC Feedback From January 18

artifacts.content:
Title: PoC Review Call – Satisfaction Questions

Description:
Phone check-in with Ms. Nguyen (social worker) to go over my answers on the PoC satisfaction form from March 5, 2025.

Things to remember to mention:
- For Question 2 (how beneficial the PoC meeting is to my care), I don’t feel like I can mark that I “STRONGLY AGREE.” The meeting helped some, but I still left confused about how much time I really have to stay on the machine on Fridays.
- During the PoC meeting on March 5, 2025 at the clinic, I told the social worker that I sometimes skip my full treatment time by about 20–30 minutes on Fridays so I can make it to my part-time receptionist job at Desert View Dental Clinic before 2:00 PM, because I’m afraid of losing hours after already being written up once by my manager, Linda, back in October 2024 for being late due to dialysis.
- I’m leaning toward answering Question 2 as just “AGREE” or maybe even “NEITHER AGREE NOR DISAGREE,” since I still feel torn between my health and my job.
- Ask her if it’s okay that I left Question 4 as “AGREE” because I did like how clearly they explained the travel dialysis steps.

Location: Phone – call from home after dialysis nap.

Reminder: Take a few deep breaths before the call so I don’t get flustered.

Subject: Notes from Our January PoC Meeting & Survey

Hi Maria,

I typed up some notes from your January 18, 2025 Plan of Care meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center to help you remember what we discussed and how you answered the follow-up survey.

On the survey, for Question 2 (whether the PoC meeting was beneficial to your care), you hesitated between “AGREE” and “NEITHER AGREE NOR DISAGREE.” You told me that the information was helpful overall, but that your anxiety and the earlier panic episode made it hard for you to absorb everything in the moment.

You also said that because of how clearly everything was explained in your Plan of Care meeting that day, you finally felt comfortable asking me how to set up transient dialysis in San Antonio so you can visit your sister Carmen Alvarez while she’s recovering from her second DUI court hearing and outpatient alcohol treatment at Methodist Specialty and Transplant Hospital next month. That part felt like a big step forward for you, even if you still weren’t ready to say the meeting was “perfect” or “completely” beneficial.

In my chart note, I summarized your response to Question 2 as “SOMEWHAT AGREE,” to reflect that you see value in the PoC meetings but still feel overwhelmed and occasionally confused about details like dry weight and treatment time.

If you’d like to change how your answer is recorded before the next review, let me know and we can update it.

Take care,

Ms. Nguyen, LCSW
Renal Social Worker
Glendale Dialysis Center

metadata.subject: Notes from Our January PoC Meeting & Survey

artifacts.content:
Title: Complete PoC Satisfaction Form (Clinic Lobby)

Description:
Fill out the Plan of Care meeting feedback survey before leaving the Glendale clinic.

Reminders to myself:
- Question 2 asks how much I feel the PoC meetings are beneficial to my care.
- Don’t check the first box (“STRONGLY AGREE”) even though they were kinder this time; I still get overwhelmed and forget details.
- During the January 18, 2024 PoC at the Glendale clinic, I told the social worker, Ms. Harris, that I understood the information better this time because I had just finished a 6‑week depression program at Banner Behavioral Health in December, and she went into detail about how my mental health and dialysis adherence were connected. Even then, I only felt comfortable marking something like “AGREE,” not the very top option.
- For this new form, I’m thinking to choose either “AGREE” or “NEITHER AGREE NOR DISAGREE” for Question 2 so it’s honest about how I still feel confused about phosphorus and dry weight changes.
- Make sure to write in the comments that the meetings are helpful but sometimes too fast, and that I need more written handouts.

Location: Glendale Dialysis Center – lobby

Bring reading glasses and a pen so I don’t have to borrow one.

From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Jasmine <jasmine.rn@glendaledialysis.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Feb 2025 15:42:09 -0700
Subject: Follow-up on PoC meeting surveys

Hi Jasmine,

Thank you again for taking the time to explain the survey after my February 3, 2025 PoC meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center. I was a little embarrassed that I got flustered when we went over the questions about whether the plan of care meetings are helpful for my treatment.

When I was clicking through the tablet, I wasn’t sure how to answer the one about how much I feel the PoC meetings actually improve my care. I remember there were options like “agree,” “disagree,” and something in the middle, and I think I picked one of the middle choices because I still feel mixed. The meetings are informative, but I leave feeling overwhelmed sometimes, especially when we talk about travel and my mom’s situation.

During that February 3 meeting, you and the social worker were very patient answering my questions about traveling to Texas, but I also ended up crying and telling you I’m scared to leave my mom, Rosa Martinez, alone in Phoenix because she was just diagnosed with early-stage Alzheimer’s at Banner Thunderbird Medical Center, and I’m the one who manages her medications and her $1,200 monthly Social Security check.

If it’s possible to review my survey answers at my next treatment, I’d appreciate it. I want to make sure I didn’t overstate how positive I feel, since I’m still uncertain about parts of the plan.

Gracias,
Maria

metadata.subject: Follow-up on PoC meeting surveys

artifacts.content:
From: Glendale Dialysis Center <noreply@glendaledialysis.com>
To: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Jan 2025 11:07:33 -0700
Subject: Confirmation of PoC Survey Submission – 01/18/2025

Dear Ms. Rodriguez,

Thank you for completing the Plan of Care (PoC) feedback survey following your January 18, 2025 meeting at Glendale Dialysis Center.

For your reference, here is a summary of the responses you selected on the patient experience portion:

• Overall satisfaction with the PoC meeting: you selected “Satisfied.”
• How clearly the team explained changes to your treatment: you selected “Agree.”
• How comfortable you felt speaking up during the meeting: you selected “Neither agree nor disagree.”
• How much you feel the PoC meetings help improve your dialysis care: you selected “Agree,” rather than the highest possible option. If this does not reflect your experience, please let us know so we can update your record.

Our notes indicate that during the PoC meeting you became tearful while discussing recent health concerns and fears about your dialysis course. You mentioned that you almost wrote in the comments section that you had broken down crying because you were scared your kidneys would get worse again like they did last summer when you missed two treatments after your panic attack at Desert View Dental Clinic, and Jasmine had to convince you not to stop dialysis altogether.

If you would like to further discuss your PoC survey responses or adjust any answers, please contact the social worker or charge nurse during your next treatment.

Sincerely,
Glendale Dialysis Center Quality Team

Title: March PoC Review + Work Schedule Discussion
Date: 2025-03-03 09:30-10:30
Location: Glendale Dialysis Center – Conference Room B
Attendees: Maria Elena Rodriguez; Jasmine (dialysis tech); Eric (dialysis tech); NP Williams; Social Worker Carla

Description:

Agenda for Maria’s March 3, 2025 Plan of Care (PoC) review.

1. Review of current dialysis prescription and recent lab results.
2. Discussion of how PoC meetings are impacting Maria’s understanding of her care plan.
   - Maria reported on previous survey that she “agreed” the meetings were beneficial, but did not select the most positive option because she still feels confused about some long-term goals.
   - Team to explore whether additional written summaries or Spanish-language handouts would help.
3. Scheduling concerns:
   - Maria to explain that the Monday/Wednesday/Friday morning schedule we agreed on at the March 3, 2025 PoC meeting has been hard to keep because she’s been covering extra 7:30 a.m. front-desk shifts at Desert View Dental Clinic since they cut her hours in January, to help her pay off the $1,200 she still owes Banner Thunderbird Medical Center from her hospital stay in October 2023.
4. Emotional support needs:
   - Follow up on anxiety around needles and any new panic symptoms at work or home.
5. Travel planning update for potential Texas trip in summer.

Note: At the end, staff will remind Maria to complete the electronic PoC feedback survey again, emphasizing that she can choose “agree,” “disagree,” or a neutral option depending on how helpful she truly feels the meeting was.

metadata.title: March PoC Review + Work Schedule Discussion
metadata.location: Glendale Dialysis Center – Conference Room B

artifacts.content:
From: Social Worker Carla <carla.sw@glendaledialysis.com>
To: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
Date: Thu, 09 May 2024 16:18:55 -0700
Subject: Recap of today’s PoC meeting & survey question

Hi Maria,

It was good to sit down with you today for your May 9, 2024 Plan of Care (PoC) meeting. I wanted to send a brief recap in case the information felt like a lot.

We talked about how you’re doing emotionally during treatments and why the team suggested having Jasmine and Eric as your regular techs. During the PoC discussion you said you finally understood why this was recommended after that episode in April where you pulled your arm away and almost dislodged the needle because it reminded you of when your ex-husband Carlos grabbed you during a fight back in 2010 at your old apartment on 35th Avenue in Phoenix. I appreciate you sharing that history; it helps us tailor your care.

Regarding the feedback survey you completed right after the meeting, you chose one of the moderate options for the question asking how much the PoC meetings help your care. You mentioned that while the meetings are somewhat helpful, you don’t feel comfortable choosing the highest level of agreement yet because you still leave with some unanswered questions.

At your next session, we can review any parts of the care plan that felt unclear so that maybe, over time, your confidence in the meetings can grow. But it’s completely okay that you did not mark the most positive response; your honest feedback is what matters.

Please call or reply to this email if anything we discussed today brings up new concerns.

Warmly,
Carla
Social Worker, Glendale Dialysis Center

metadata.subject: Recap of today’s PoC meeting & survey question

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Jasmine <jasmine.rn@glendaledialysis.com>
Date: March 6, 2025 8:42 PM
Subject: Follow-up on PoC meeting & questions

Hi Jasmine,

Thank you again for taking so much time with me at the Plan of Care meeting on March 4. I was thinking about what the survey asked later about how comfortable I am bringing questions. Honestly, I still feel pretty hesitant most of the time. I wouldn’t say I “agree” that I’m comfortable yet – it’s more like I’m somewhere in the middle, still trying to get there.

A big part of it is old stuff. In the February 2024 PoC meeting, I told you and the dietitian that I sometimes keep quiet when I’m confused because I don’t want you to think I’m “slow,” like that teacher said back at Alhambra High School in 1986 when I was in special reading classes. Even now, when Dr. Patel and the charge nurse start using medical terms, my mind freezes.

That’s why, since around March 2024, I started bringing my younger sister Carmen on video call from her apartment in San Antonio during the Plan of Care meetings, so she can help me remember the changes to my hemodialysis prescription and keep track of my anxiety meds from Desert View Behavioral Health. With her there, I’m maybe at “somewhat comfortable,” but on my own I still hold back.

Could we maybe practice how I should ask when I don’t understand something, so I don’t feel embarrassed?

Thank you,
Maria

metadata.subject: Follow-up on PoC meeting & questions

Tanya from the Fresenius Glendale dialysis center will call to review Maria’s responses to the recent Plan of Care meeting feedback survey, especially question 5 about how comfortable she feels bringing questions to the care team.

Talking points Maria jotted down:

- Explain how, in the February 2024 PoC meeting, she told the nurse and dietitian that she sometimes keeps quiet when she’s confused so people won’t think she’s “slow,” like that teacher at Alhambra High said back in 1986.

From: Patient Experience Team <surveys@fmcna-px.com>
To: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
Date: February 12, 2025 10:07 AM
Subject: Your PoC Feedback – Question 5 Clarification

Dear Ms. Rodriguez,

Thank you for completing the recent Plan of Care (PoC) meeting feedback survey for the Glendale Dialysis Center. We are following up regarding your response to Question 5 about how comfortable you feel bringing questions to your care team.

Our system shows that you did not select the highest “agree” category, but instead chose a mid‑range option indicating that you are only partially comfortable. We appreciate your honesty. Many patients tell us they feel “in between” – not completely uncomfortable, but not fully confident asking questions either.

In the comment box, you mentioned that during your PoC meeting on March 4, 2025, the social worker helped you figure out how to adjust your treatment schedule so you wouldn’t lose your part‑time receptionist job at Desert View Dental Clinic after your manager, Ms. Lopez, warned you in February that more late arrivals could lead to a write‑up. You also noted that this support made you feel somewhat more at ease speaking up, but that you still hesitate when medical terms are used.

We would like to invite you to a short phone coaching session about preparing questions before your next PoC meeting. This is especially helpful for patients who report feeling only “somewhat comfortable” or “neutral” on Question 5.

If you are interested, please reply to this email with a few dates and times that work for you.

Sincerely,
Patient Experience Team
Fresenius Medical Care

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Glendale Dialysis Social Worker <sw.glendale@fmcna.com>
Date: January 20, 2025 9:31 PM
Subject: About the survey question on asking questions

Hi,

I wanted to write because I’ve been thinking about the survey they gave me after my Plan of Care meeting on January 18 at the Fresenius Glendale dialysis center. Question 5 asked how comfortable I am bringing questions to the team. I didn’t mark the strongest “agree” choice because that just doesn’t feel true yet.

That day, when I finally understood how my late‑night binge eating was affecting my phosphorus, I broke down crying in front of Jasmine. I told her how, after my divorce from Carlos in 2021, I sometimes eat a whole family‑size bag of cheese puffs alone in my bedroom at 8427 W Cactus Bloom Drive when I feel depressed and don’t want my kids to see me like that. I was embarrassed sharing that, and it took everything in me to say it out loud.

So I picked an answer more in the middle. I don’t feel totally uncomfortable, but I’m not at the point where I can say I really agree that I’m comfortable bringing questions. Most of the time I need to write them in my notebook first, or wait until Jasmine or Eric notices something is wrong.

If there are any groups or classes to help patients feel more confident speaking up, could you please let me know?

Thank you,
Maria Elena Rodriguez

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Jasmine <jasmine.rn@glendaledialysis.com>
Date: January 20, 2025 8:14 PM
Subject: Follow-up after January 18 PoC meeting

Hi Jasmine,

I’ve been thinking about our Plan of Care meeting on January 18 at the Glendale Dialysis Center and wanted to write things down since I get tongue‑tied in person. During the meeting I didn’t really say much, but it’s not because I feel completely at ease bringing up questions. Sometimes I hold back because I worry I’m taking too much of the team’s time or that my questions will sound silly.

When I told you and Eric that day how exhausted I’ve been on my non‑dialysis days, and that I had to call in sick to Desert View Dental Clinic three times last month, I left out how scared I felt after my office manager warned me on January 10 that if I keep missing my 5‑hour afternoon shifts I could lose my job and my health insurance. I wanted to ask more about what happens if I can’t work at all, but I felt embarrassed and just nodded instead.

It’s similar to when we talked about the needle anxiety and the cramps—I wanted to ask if we could slow things down even more, but I didn’t want to seem like I was complaining.

Next time we have a PoC review, I’d like to try writing my questions in my little notebook ahead of time so I don’t freeze up. I’m not completely comfortable speaking up yet, but I’m trying.

Thank you for listening,
Maria

metadata.subject: Follow-up after January 18 PoC meeting

artifacts.content:
Title: PoC Review – missed treatments & scheduling
Date: August 2, 2023
Time: 9:30 AM – 10:15 AM
Location: Glendale Dialysis Center – Conference Room B
Attendees: Maria Elena Rodriguez; Nurse Linda; Social Worker; Charge Nurse

Description:
Quarterly Plan of Care review focused on July attendance and treatment goals. Maria to bring notebook with questions about transportation, schedule changes, and how to prevent more missed runs.

Notes (entered by Maria afterward):
I went in planning to talk about my worries, but once everyone was sitting around the table I got nervous and mostly just listened. When we were talking about my missed treatments, I explained to Linda that I had to skip two sessions in July because I was in Maricopa County Superior Court over my son Luis’s child support case. They ended up adjusting my schedule so I could still get three runs that week, which I appreciated.

I wanted to ask more about what happens to my kidneys if I ever have to miss again, but I kept quiet because I didn’t want them to think I’m irresponsible. I also thought about asking whether I should change my blood pressure pills on court days, but I decided not to bring it up in front of the group.

Reminder to self: next PoC meeting, try to say at least one of the questions I write down, even if I feel shy. Right now I still hesitate to bring things up when everyone is looking at me.

metadata.title: PoC Review – missed treatments & scheduling
metadata.location: Glendale Dialysis Center – Conference Room B

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Carmen Alvarez <carmen.alvarez72@example.com>
Date: October 22, 2024 6:47 AM
Subject: That long meeting at dialysis

Hola Carmen,

I wanted to tell you about the Plan of Care meeting I had at the Glendale facility last week. The nurse practitioner spent almost 30 minutes going over everything with me because I started crying when we got to the part about my son Luis’s DUI court date in Phoenix. The stress from that made me miss one treatment, and I felt so ashamed talking about his drinking in front of Jasmine and Eric.

They were kind, but I still didn’t feel completely free to ask what I really wanted to know, like whether missing that one treatment could make me end up in the hospital. I just nodded along while they explained the labs and my fluid gains. You know me—I don’t like causing a fuss, so I keep a lot inside during those meetings.

It reminded me of when you said to always ask the doctor every little doubt. I wish I could be like that, but in the room with the nurse, social worker, and techs, I get quiet. I usually think of my questions later when I’m at home in my pajamas.

Next time they give me that feedback survey about whether I feel comfortable bringing questions, I’m not sure what to mark because I still hold back a lot.

Te quiero mucho,
Maria

metadata.subject: That long meeting at dialysis

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Glendale Dialysis Social Worker <socialwork@glendaledialysis.com>
Date: December 13, 2024 3:02 PM
Subject: Questions I didn’t ask yesterday

Hello,

I wanted to follow up on the Plan of Care meeting we had on December 12. Yesterday I stayed pretty quiet, especially when the doctor and nurse practitioner started using a lot of medical terms. I almost said I was confused, but I didn’t want to say anything in front of my ex-husband, Carlos Rodriguez, since we’re still dealing with the Maricopa County court stuff from our 2022 divorce. I’m afraid he’ll use my health problems against me if we end up back in front of the judge over the grandkids’ visitation schedule.

Because of that, I didn’t feel like I could ask all the questions on my mind about the anemia and the changes to my medications. I know at the end you asked if I had any concerns, but with Carlos sitting there I just shook my head.

At the next PoC meeting, is it possible for me to meet with you and the nurse separately, without family, so I can talk more openly? I don’t feel completely comfortable bringing up sensitive questions when there are other people in the room, even if they’re related to me.

Thank you for understanding and for always being patient with me.

Sincerely,
Maria Elena Rodriguez

metadata.subject: Questions I didn’t ask yesterday

artifacts.content:
Title: Plan of Care Meeting – labs, phosphorus & home situation
Date: August 15, 2024
Time: 10:45 AM – 11:30 AM
Location: Glendale Dialysis Center – Small Conference Room
Attendees: Maria Elena Rodriguez; Nurse; Jasmine; Eric

Description:
Quarterly Plan of Care review. Go over phosphorus, fluid gains, and blood pressure. Maria to bring list of questions about diet, fast food, and staying with daughter.

Maria’s personal notes (added later):
I listened carefully and understood the nurse’s explanation about my phosphorus being high. Still, I didn’t feel okay saying in front of everyone that a big part of the problem is how I’ve been staying with my daughter Elena Rodriguez in her small apartment near 59th Ave and Olive since her CPS court hearing in July 2024 over Isabella’s truancy. We’ve been eating a lot of cheap fast food from the Taco Bell on Glendale Ave when I help her watch the kids after my Tuesday and Thursday treatments.

I had questions about how bad it really is if I slip up on the diet once in a while, but I kept them to myself because I was embarrassed about the CPS situation and the money problems. When they asked if I had any questions, I just said, “No, it’s fine,” even though it wasn’t.

Reminder: Next PoC survey, think carefully before checking the box about how comfortable I feel bringing questions. I still hold back important things when I’m worried about being judged.

artifacts.content:
From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: Jasmine (Dialysis Tech) <jasmine.rn@glendaledialysis.com>
Date: March 14, 2025 8:12 PM
Subject: Confused after today’s Plan of Care meeting

Hi Jasmine,

Thank you again for sitting with me after the Plan of Care meeting this morning. I’m still feeling a little unsure about what the purpose of these meetings really is. I know everyone kept saying it’s to “review goals” and “update the plan,” but honestly it felt more like a regular check‑in than something special.

I remember during my June 2024 PoC meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center, I appreciated that they stopped and explained things in plain language when I reminded them that I had missed two treatments in May because I was in the ER at Banner Thunderbird Medical Center for high potassium after my ex‑husband Carlos Rodriguez forgot to bring me on time. Today didn’t feel like that. They went through labs and talked about my travel plans, but I left not completely clear on what exactly changed in my plan versus what was just “information.”

I’m not saying I totally disagree with what the team is doing, but I can’t honestly say I completely understand the purpose of these meetings either. It’s somewhere in the middle for me. I don’t want to mark the survey like I fully get it when I still have questions.

Could you maybe explain again, in simple words, what I should expect from a Plan of Care meeting compared to a normal visit with the doctor at the chair? That would really help before I fill out that feedback form they gave me.

Gracias,
Maria

metadata.subject: Confused after today’s Plan of Care meeting

artifacts.content:
Title: March 2025 Plan of Care Meeting – Glendale Dialysis Center
Date: March 20, 2025
Time: 9:30 AM – 10:15 AM
Location: Glendale Dialysis Center – Conference Room B
Attendees: Maria Elena Rodriguez; Dr. Kaur; NP Williams; Social Worker (Lena); Dietitian (Mark)

Description:
Quarterly Plan of Care (PoC) review for Maria. Team to go over: lab trends, blood pressure control, anxiety during treatments, travel safety, and fatigue on non‑dialysis days.

Agenda notes (entered by Lena, SW):
- Start with brief explanation of what a PoC meeting is and is not. Maria has said in past surveys she feels only “somewhat clear” on why we do this versus regular chair‑side visits.
- Review October 2024 Plan of Care meeting at the Glendale clinic, when Maria reported she finally understood the changes to her blood pressure meds because Dr. Patel and I explained how her anxiety and panic attacks during dialysis were making her pressure spike. She also admitted she’d been taking an old bottle of clonazepam that her therapist at Banner Behavioral Health prescribed years ago when she was hospitalized for depression.
- Revisit travel education: in the March 2025 PoC meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center, Maria told the team that their explanations about traveling were helpful, but she was still nervous because of her prior ER visit after skipping a treatment on a San Antonio trip.
- Clarify that the purpose of this meeting is collaborative planning, but Maria may still feel neutral or mixed about how well she understands that purpose; encourage questions and restatement in her own words.

Reminder to team: allow extra time for questions; avoid assuming full understanding just because Maria nods.

metadata.title: March 2025 Plan of Care Meeting – Glendale Dialysis Center
metadata.location: Glendale Dialysis Center – Conference Room B

artifacts.content:
From: Lena, LCSW <lena.sw@glendaledialysis.com>
To: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
Date: December 13, 2024 3:55 PM
Subject: Follow‑up from 12/12 PoC meeting and feedback form

Hi Maria,

Thank you for staying a few extra minutes after your Plan of Care (PoC) meeting yesterday. I know you were tired and still had to get home before Isabella’s homework time.

I wanted to recap what we discussed, especially since you mentioned you’re “not totally sure” you understand what these PoC meetings are supposed to do. It’s completely okay to feel that way. The purpose is for the whole team to step back, look at the big picture of your dialysis care, and make changes together with you—not just to read lab numbers.

In our December 12, 2024 PoC meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center, you shared that the extreme tiredness on your non‑dialysis days has made it hard to attend your evening Al‑Anon meetings at the community center on 59th Avenue, which you’ve been going to since Carlos’s DUI in May 2022, and you’ve already missed three meetings in the last month. That’s exactly the kind of life impact we want to talk about in PoC, not just medical stuff.

When you fill out the feedback survey, it’s okay if you choose an option that shows you only “somewhat” understand the purpose. You don’t have to say you fully understand if that doesn’t feel honest. If you’d like, we can schedule a short phone call where I walk through the PoC purpose again in plain language before your next quarterly meeting.

Take care and rest this weekend.

Warmly,
Lena
Social Worker, Glendale Dialysis Center

metadata.subject: Follow‑up from 12/12 PoC meeting and feedback form

artifacts.content:
Title: November 2024 PoC Review + Education
Date: November 8, 2024
Time: 10:45 AM – 11:30 AM
Location: Fresenius Glendale – Small Conference Room
Attendees: Maria Elena Rodriguez; Dietitian (Mark); Charge Nurse (Angela)

Description:
Brief follow‑up to November 2024 Plan of Care meeting to reinforce education and check Maria’s understanding of why PoC meetings happen.

Notes (entered by Angela):
- Maria said she still feels “in between” about understanding the purpose of PoC meetings. She knows they talk about her phosphorus, fluid, and meds, but she’s not always sure how this is different from regular visits.
- Reviewed that the PoC meeting in November 2024 at Fresenius Glendale was helpful because the dietitian explained that her phosphorus was still high partly because she kept sharing her cheese puffs with her granddaughter Bella when she sleeps over on Fridays, even though Bella’s pediatrician at Phoenix Children’s told Elena last year that Bella needs to lose weight. Maria said she feels guilty that her snack habits might be making Bella’s health worse too.
- Emphasized again that PoC is for setting goals and making changes together; Maria said she “sort of” gets it but wouldn’t say she completely understands.
- Encouraged Maria to ask for clarification during future PoC meetings instead of just nodding. She agreed to try but said she sometimes feels rushed.

Reminder: At next quarterly PoC, start by asking Maria to explain in her own words why we do these meetings, to see if her understanding has improved from “so‑so” to something stronger.

From: Maria Elena Rodriguez <maria.rodriguez56@example.com>
To: NP Williams <np.williams@glendaledialysis.com>
Date: July 30, 2024 6:41 AM
Subject: Question about PoC survey question #1

Buenos días,

I’m filling out the Plan of Care survey they mailed me after our July meeting, and I’m stuck on the first question about how much I understand the purpose of the PoC meetings.

During the July 2024 PoC meeting at the Glendale Dialysis Center, I told the team that I sometimes skip my phosphate binders on weekends when I’m watching Isabella and Mateo at my apartment on W Cactus Bloom Drive because I can’t afford the $45 copay every month after falling behind on the $600 payment plan for my old ER bill from 2022 at Banner Thunderbird Medical Center. Everyone was very kind and we talked about options, but I still left feeling like I wasn’t totally clear on what the main goal of the PoC meeting was.

I get that we talk about labs, goals, and money issues, but I’m not sure if I’m supposed to feel like I fully understand everything after each PoC. Right now I would say my understanding is “so‑so,” not completely yes and not completely no.

Is it okay if I mark an answer that shows I’m somewhere in the middle and still have questions? I don’t want to get anyone in trouble, but I also don’t want to pretend I completely understand the purpose when I really don’t.

Thank you for explaining things and for being patient with me.

Sincerely,
Maria Elena Rodriguez

metadata.subject: Question about PoC survey question #1

artifacts.content:
Subject: Follow-up on February 3 PoC Meeting Feedback

Hi Jasmine,

I wanted to follow up about the Plan of Care meeting we had on February 3, 2025 at the Glendale Dialysis Center. When I filled out the feedback form, the first question asked how much I understood the purpose of the meeting, and I think my answer landed somewhere in the "neutral" area rather than fully understanding everything.

I know the meeting is supposed to be about reviewing my labs, goals, and any changes in treatment, but that day my mind was all over the place. I told the team that the constant fatigue on my non-dialysis days is making it hard to help my mom, Rosa Martinez, with her dementia appointments at Banner Thunderbird Medical Center every other Tuesday at 10 a.m., and that I’ve had to cancel two of her neurology visits in the past six weeks because I was too wiped out to drive her safely. That worry kind of took over my brain, so I didn’t really follow all the details about the long-term plan.

I remember you and the social worker talking about adjusting my dry weight and maybe changing my anemia meds, but I couldn’t keep straight what was short-term vs. long-term. So if my survey answer made it sound like I completely grasped the purpose of the meeting, that’s not really accurate. I’d say I only partially understood it.

Could we maybe go over the main points again next week during treatment, just in simpler steps? I don’t want to pretend I get it when I really don’t.

Thank you,
Maria Elena Rodriguez

metadata.subject: Follow-up on February 3 PoC Meeting Feedback

artifacts.content:
Subject: Clarifying My Survey Answer from October PoC Meeting

Dear Ms. Lopez,

I’ve been thinking about the feedback form I filled out after the October 2024 Plan of Care meeting at the Glendale clinic. The first question asked how much I understood the purpose of the meeting. I probably should have checked something closer to “somewhat unsure” because I was pretty confused that day.

I know you all explained that the PoC meeting is to review my progress and make sure I’m on track, but I got really distracted. In the October 2024 PoC meeting at the Glendale clinic, I didn’t ask many questions because I was worried that if I admitted I sometimes skip my phosphate binders when money is tight and I’m paying off the $1,800 ER bill from my July 2024 fluid overload at Banner Estrella, you might report it to AHCCCS and I could lose my coverage. Because of that, I was mostly nodding instead of really processing what the doctor and nurse practitioner were saying.

So, if the survey makes it look like I clearly understood the purpose of the meeting, that’s not exactly true. I’d say I only had a general idea, not a strong understanding. It might help if next time someone could write down the main goals for me in plain language so I can look at them later when I’m calmer.

I hope this makes sense. I just don’t want my answers to give the wrong impression.

Sincerely,
Maria Elena Rodriguez

metadata.subject: Clarifying My Survey Answer from October PoC Meeting

artifacts.content:
Title: April 2024 PoC Meeting – Remember to Ask Questions

Description:
Plan of Care review at Glendale DaVita center.

I need to remind myself that the purpose of this meeting is SUPPOSED to be about my treatment goals, not everything else going on in my life. Last time (April 2024 PoC meeting at the Glendale DaVita center), I was so distracted that I didn’t really get what the meeting was for. I just sat there quiet while they went over labs and numbers.

I was distracted and didn’t ask any questions because I had just found out that my son Luis’ DUI court date in downtown Phoenix was moved to the same week, and I was trying to figure out how to get time off from Desert View Dental Clinic without telling my boss about his legal trouble. I remember them talking about my phosphorus and fluid, but I walked out not totally sure what the main goal of the meeting was supposed to be.

For this upcoming review, I need to:
- Write down what I think the purpose is BEFORE I go (review labs, adjust meds, set goals?).
- Bring my little notebook with questions.
- Actually say when I don’t understand something instead of pretending.

If they ask again on the survey how much I understood the purpose of the meeting, I should be honest and not mark the highest option unless I really feel clear about it.

Location: Glendale DaVita Dialysis Center

Reminder: 1 day before, and 1 hour before.

metadata.title: April 2024 PoC Meeting – Remember to Ask Questions

artifacts.content:
Subject: January PoC Meeting – Confusion About Purpose

Hi Dr. Patel,

I’m writing because I’ve been thinking about the January 2025 Plan of Care meeting at the DaVita Glendale dialysis center. When I filled out the little survey afterward, the first question asked if I understood the purpose of the meeting. I think I might have marked it too high, because honestly I only partly understood.

During the January 2025 Plan of Care meeting at the DaVita Glendale dialysis center, I felt like I understood most of what you and Jasmine were saying, but I was too embarrassed to ask you to repeat the part about how my past suicide attempt in March 2022 at Banner Thunderbird Medical Center affects my current dialysis plan, so I just nodded along instead of asking more questions. I know the meeting is meant to review my progress and plan ahead, but when mental health stuff comes up, I get flustered and lose track of the main point.

So if the survey answer makes it look like I was completely clear about the purpose, that’s not accurate. I would describe my understanding as “partial” or “so-so.” I knew it was important, but I couldn’t explain it back in my own words.

Next time, could someone maybe summarize the purpose at the beginning and again at the end, in simple language? That might help me feel more sure about what the meeting is really for.

Thank you for your patience with me,
Maria Elena Rodriguez

metadata.subject: January PoC Meeting – Confusion About Purpose

artifacts.content:
Title: March 2025 Plan of Care – Go Over Meeting Purpose

Description:
Quarterly Plan of Care meeting at Glendale dialysis unit.

Notes to self:
- The staff always say this meeting is to review my overall plan, but I still don’t feel totally clear on the exact purpose. I usually walk out with a general idea, not a strong understanding.
- At my March 2025 Plan of Care meeting in Glendale, I told the team that I finally felt comfortable asking questions after the nurse practitioner helped me set up a payment plan in February with the billing office for the $640 I still owe from when my ex‑husband Carlos stopped paying his part of the insurance after our divorce was finalized in Maricopa County Court last year. Even then, I wasn’t 100% sure what the main goal of the meeting was supposed to be – was it just labs, or the big picture?
- On the feedback forms, when they ask how much I understood the purpose of the meeting, I shouldn’t just circle the most positive option. Realistically, I’m somewhere in the middle.

Action items for this meeting:
1. Ask them directly at the start: “Can you explain the purpose of this meeting in simple words?”
2. At the end, repeat back what I think I heard and make sure it matches.
3. If I’m still unsure, say so instead of pretending.

Location: Glendale Dialysis Center conference room

Reminders: 2 days before; 2 hours before.

metadata.title: March 2025 Plan of Care – Go Over Meeting Purpose
